text: Robotic repair of a recurrent lumbar hernia. The objective of this presentation is to provide the learner with the operative approach and technique for robotic repair of a recurrent lumbar hernia. Lumbar hernias can form in either the inferior lumbar triangle, which lies superficially, or the superior lumbar triangle, which is deep. Of the two, the superior triangle is more commonly the site of herniation. The superior lumbar triangle is formed medially by the sacrospinalis, laterally by the posterior border of the internal abdominal oblique, and superiorly by the twelfth rib. The floor of the superior lumbar triangle is the transversalis fascia, and its roof is the external abdominal oblique. The margins of the inferior lumbar triangle are comprised of the iliac crest, latissimus dorsi, and external abdominal oblique. The floor of the inferior lumbar triangle is the internal abdominal oblique.

Our patient is a fifty-five year old woman with a BMI of twenty-one point three. who presented with a recurrent left posterior bulge. She reported that she had a posterior lumbar hernia repair in in that did not involve mesh. The hernia recurred shortly thereafter, but she did not seek treatment for several years. However, it started to enlarge and become more symptomatic, so she has now requested surgical repair. Preoperative abdominal CT shows a fat-containing, two point seven by two point five centimeter left lumbar hernia.

The patient is positioned in the steep reverse Trendelenburg in the right lateral decubitus position with arms outstretched. A 12mm trocar is inserted in the left upper quadrant using the Hassan technique, and the abdomen is insufflated. An 8mm robotic camera with a 30-degree angle is inserted, followed by three additional 8mm trocars along the left hemiabdomen. The peritoneum is incised lateral to the colonic reflection, and dissection proceeds through retroperitoneal fat to identify the transversus abdominis muscle. The dissection extends cranially onto the diaphragm and into the pelvis, preserving the subcostal nerve. Retroperitoneal fat is visible herniating through the posterior lumbar defect, with the hernia edges clearly defined.

The surgeon cuts and removes a stitch from the prior repair, revealing a defect measuring three by two centimeters. An attempt to close the defect with a 1-0 V-lock suture is shown, but the suture pulls through the friable tissue. A 7.5 by 15 centimeter barred soft mesh is then placed into the abdominal cavity, centered over the defect with overlap in all directions. The mesh is secured using a 2-0 Vicryl stitch and is visualized to ensure it lies completely flat in the posterior space.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum with a running two-oh V-lock suture, completing the repair. The final visualization shows the repaired area with no visible defects.
